chunk_summary: Guidance on managing food refusal, neophobia, and identifying feeding disorder warning signs in infants and toddlers, emphasizing structured mealtime practices, repeated exposure to new foods, and recognizing red flags requiring evaluation.
learning_objectives: Understand developmental phases of food refusal and neophobia in infants and toddlers.
Learn strategies for structured mealtime practices and repeated exposure to new foods.
Identify red flags indicating feeding disorders and conditions requiring multidisciplinary evaluation.
key_facts: Food neophobia often occurs during texture changes at 8–12 months and toddlerhood.
New foods may require 8–10 neutral exposures before acceptance.
Toddlers grazing or sipping milk/juice throughout the day may lack hunger at meals.
Red flags include poor weight gain, choking, vomiting, breathlessness, severe constipation, or mealtimes exceeding 30 minutes.
Extreme selectivity, sensory aversion past 10 months, or developmental delays may indicate feeding disorders.
reference_quote: A new food may need 8–10 neutral offerings on different days before acceptance. (Source: https://www.healthychildren.org/English/healthy-living/nutrition/Pages/Picky-Eaters.aspx)
document_key_topics: Food refusal and neophobia in infants and toddlers
Structured mealtime practices and exposure to new foods
Warning signs of feeding disorders and conditions requiring evaluation
